Transcrição Completa: 64 Application Funnel Questions Best Practices 12 14 24   [00:00] "All right. So, today we're going through application question best practices. Uh, again, I mentioned this at the beginning of the call, but let me just reiterate it now. So, one thing that I think is really silly is when a business looks for best practices for application questions from other random businesses."   [00:20] "That's one of the most common questions I get from you guys when you guys are looking to build out your applications. You say something along the lines of, 'Hey, can you send me some people who are doing well with CallFunnels? I want to copy their application.'"   [00:35] "And the reason I think that's so silly is every single business that does well with their application data is leveraging things that their salespeople specifically will use against the leads on a sales call to increase the probability of that person closing."   [01:00] "In addition to that, the questions are used to disqualify the wrong people to not allow them to schedule at all, which forces the right people to get sent back to the pixel for long-term pixel conditioning, which as we know has a tremendously positive effect for the pixel."   [01:20] "So, to be clear, it's a two-fold lesson. The simple one is the qualifying questions that again disapprove the wrong people and make sure they never touch that pixel. The bigger way to look at it and this is really important is what are the right questions that your salespeople actually give a shit about."   [01:45] "Right now I want you to do a simple thing for me. Put a one in the chat if your closers actually currently use application data when they're on a sales call with somebody, they bring it up. Put a two in the chat if they do not use application data."   [02:15] "So, interestingly enough, we got about a half and half about half ones and half twos. So, here's the thing. First of all, for the people that are saying one, are you saying one because you actually have listened to the sales calls and with certainty know that they are actually leveraging the application data?"   [02:35] "One thing that we commonly find is that the people who actually think their application data is being used, they're not. The salespeople have some type of flow that they found works best for them."   [02:50]  📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ─────────────────────────────  TEXTO:  "Application Data Usage Problem"   NÚMEROS:  50% of businesses think data is being used, 50% acknowledge it's not   VISUAL:  Split comparison visual mostrando marketing team vs sales team, desconexão entre criação de perguntas e uso real pelos vendedores, ícone de formulário de aplicação separado de ícone de telefone de vendas   [03:10] "Jeff, unmute yourself and tell me why your leads, like why aren't your sales people currently leveraging the application data you gather?"   [03:20] "Uh, well, right now we're just asking for monthly revenue and then we basically just state the upfront investment or the total investment of the program. Um, honestly, I haven't been doing it long enough."   [03:35] "You're making people money with your offer?"   [03:38] "I think so, 100%."   [03:40] "And people who are making a certain amount of money, you don't think that it's smart to leverage that against them in their sales process?"   [03:48] "No, I definitely think it is."   [03:50] "What if they are making a certain amount of money? How much easier do you think it is when somebody comes through my application that's already making half a mill a month to get them to a mill a month comparatively to the person who's at 100K a month?"   [04:05] "Much easier. Much easier."   [04:08] "Right. And you don't think I'm going to leverage that in that sales process to get the person to buy?"   [04:15] "No, I think it's I think 100% leveraging that because they already have so much momentum in systems that you can just come in and add gas. 100%."   [04:25] "Axel, unmute yourself and tell me why your people currently are not using your application data in the sales process."   [04:32] "Honestly, I just think, man, I'm like revamping the whole sales. I'm fighting everybody, restarting from scratch with the sales team. Like, I need to get rid of fat guy closers and get rid of people in straight up."   [04:45] "Makes sense. Are your current questions not applicable to be used by the sales team? Does the sales team not have visibility on the data? You just haven't trained them on using the application data?"   [04:58] "Actually, they are the questions are applicable. For example, some of them go over like what's your trading experience? And that would matter. Somebody who just started trading or never traded. It's not the same as somebody who also has had some experience, right?"   [05:20]  📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ─────────────────────────────  TEXTO:  "Application Best Practices - First Question"   NÚMEROS:  Question #1 priority   VISUAL:  Funil de aplicação mostrando primeira questão no topo, ícone de múltipla escolha, X vermelho sobre informações pessoais, checkmark verde sobre perguntas fáceis, setas indicando fluxo de qualificação   [05:25] "Listen, I'm going to go through a few things with you real quick just in terms of application best practices. When you look at applications, one of the biggest things that you have to look at is the first question. Okay, the first question matters a lot."   [05:40] "The first question should be something easy to answer. This question in particular is what starts the entire application process. The things that you should never do like never ever start with answers that are like personal information."   [05:55] "So never start with the personal info yet like contact details. Don't start the application with like name, email, phone, etc. It should always be a simple like multiple choice question that your sales people are going to use."   [06:10] "It should also I like it can be a qualifier, okay? But it shouldn't be the money qualifier. Should not be the money qualifier. And the reason being again just lose a lot of people right away if you started off like that."   [06:25] "Andrew, unmute yourself and tell the people about your offer real quick."   [06:30] "I have a real estate offer. We help people start sober living homes and uh they can make 10x cash flow compared to traditional rents without any licenses or certifications."   [06:42] "Got it. Okay. Now, assuming that this application would be on a VSSL page, somebody may or may not have watched it and you have a first question to ask here. What do you think your first question should be?"   [06:55] "Our first question is how many properties they own now."   [06:58] "Okay. And do your sales team in any way, shape, or form leverage that data on the sales call?"   [07:03] "Yes."   [07:05] "Good. And how do they leverage it?"   [07:08] "Um, it actually determines the direction of the call. If this is bizop for them, they've never owned an investment property. Like that's it. It's a different line of questioning than if they have a current property that they can simply convert."   [07:25]  📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ─────────────────────────────  TEXTO:  "Andrew's First Question Analysis"   NÚMEROS:  0 properties, 1-5 properties, 6+ properties, 40% have zero properties, 3.3x higher conversion rate for property owners   VISUAL:  Gráfico de barras comparando taxas de conversão, ícone de casa representando propriedades, seta ascendente mostrando diferença de conversão, layout de segmentação de leads   [07:30] "Honestly, that's a that's a baller first question to ask. I I completely agree that that makes sense. And is it a multiple choice question?"   [07:38] "Yeah. So they can choose zero, they can choose one to five, or they can choose uh six plus."   [07:45] "Do you think there's a difference in the person who owns one versus the person who owns five? Like is there a significant difference or are they about the same?"   [07:55] "It's pretty significant. Um our lead flow is about 40% say they have uh zero properties and they buy at a much lower rate. They just don't have typically they don't have a lot of cash because it's bizop."   [08:10] "The people who have at least one property buy uh 3.3 times higher rate than the ones who don't have a property."   [08:18] "Got it. And to be clear, do you think it would make more sense than to have zero, one to two, three to five? Like where's the threshold where one is less significant than somewhere up to five?"   [08:35] "Yeah and it's a much different conversation with people who own like 10 homes versus five or four they typically have a little bit more ego they care more about the numbers and return on investment than just the do good part of it."   [08:50] "Do you leverage any of this application data currently in marketing automation? So like based on how they answer, do you communicate with them in specific ways in your marketing automation?"   [09:05] "Yeah. When we confirm the appointment, we say and it's automated, it's AI, but it says, hey, you see you marked you wanted to make 10,000. So the next question is, how much do you want to make a month upon completion of the program?"   [09:25]  📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ─────────────────────────────  TEXTO:  "Evoke Advisors Case Study"   NÚMEROS:  $23 billion AUM, $1 million minimum, 46% of leads have $5M+, ranges: $1M, $5M, $10M, $25M, $50M, $100M+   VISUAL:  Pirâmide financeira mostrando diferentes níveis de wealth management, ícones de dinheiro em escala crescente, gráfico comparativo de abordagens de vendas por tier financeiro   [09:30] "Listen to this, guys. When you ask a question, I give this example in the email matrix SOP where I talk about this company. They were actually in the inner circle for a long duration of time. They're a company called Evoke Advisors."   [09:50] "Their minimum they manage $23 billion in AUM assets under management. Their minimum person that they want to manage money for has a million dollars that they want to transfer to Evoke Advisors to manage."   [10:05] "We get 46% of their leads that come through with more than a mill with more than $5 million. Their application questions range from a million, 5 million, 10 million, 25 million, 50 million, and 100 million plus."   [10:25] "Is there a substantial difference? Put a one in the chat if you agree, put a two in the chat if you disagree. Is there a substantial difference between how I should talk to somebody that only has a million dollars versus somebody that has $100 million?"   [10:45] "It's like, yeah, obviously my salespeople alone should be communicating dramatically differently. My sales process is likely going to reflect differently, right? But what else should reflect differently? How I communicate them with my automation, my emails, my texts, my hammer them strategy."   [11:05]  📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ─────────────────────────────  TEXTO:  "Pocket Opportunities Concept"   NÚMEROS:  0 properties = 40% of leads, 3.3x conversion difference   VISUAL:  Mapa de oportunidades mostrando gaps não explorados, ícones de funil com pontos de otimização destacados, setas conectando dados de aplicação a sequências de email e conteúdo hammer   [11:10] "A lot of people that first learn about my strategies, oh, they can be overwhelming to a degree because it's significantly more work than just standard direct response. So, when you just first start looking at all the different opportunities, I call them pocket opportunities."   [11:30] "As an example, let's use Andrew's offer again. If he gets somebody that has zero properties, just literally based on that singular question alone, he can create an entire email sequence, he can create an entire hammer them content sequence that is specific to the information that that person would need to know."   [11:50] "Him saying that the people who already have a property buy at 3.3 times greater levels than the people who have zero is not a flex. It is a representative statistic of his inefficiency to communicate effectively to the people with zero properties."   [12:10] "So you're telling me almost half your leads come through and don't buy from you because you fail to adapt your communication style to what they need to know to buy. That's what you're really saying."   [12:25] "40%. That's almost half."   [12:28] "Some of them do still buy. It's just there's a lot of them, man, that don't have any money. So, that's why I was asking about financial qualifiers."   [12:40]  📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ─────────────────────────────  TEXTO:  "Data-Driven Communication Strategy"   NÚMEROS:  $1M vs $100M client comparison   VISUAL:  Diagrama de fluxo mostrando segmentação de leads, múltiplos caminhos de comunicação baseados em respostas, ícones representando diferentes sequências de email e conteúdo hammer, conexão entre dados de aplicação e processo de vendas   [12:45] "Listen to this. When we get data that we can leverage on our leads, this is just the first question. By the way, we can also update the sequences that people go into based on follow-up questions that we ask."   [13:00] "We can put them into specific hammer them sequences that have content specific to how they answer. We can also tell our salespeople in our sales training lessons how to leverage specific application data against the leads to increase the probability they buy."   [13:20] "Because again, with the Evoke Advisors client, if they get somebody on the phone that has a million dollars, the guy that has a $100 million that they get on the phone, they literally live in completely different worlds."   [13:35] "They have completely different perspectives on life. They have completely different problems. They have completely different selling points. They have completely different communication styles. They're going to have different amounts of touch points in the sales process."   [13:55]  📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ─────────────────────────────  TEXTO:  "Application Question Framework"   NÚMEROS:  2 key purposes for questions   VISUAL:  Diagrama de duplo propósito mostrando: 1) Qualificação (ícone de filtro) e 2) Comunicação contextual (ícone de mensagem), setas conectando respostas a diferentes caminhos de follow-up   [14:00] "Remember when you ask an application question, it should be defined based on how you can follow up with the lead. It's twofold. It's qualifier and how we're going to communicate to them."   [14:15] "We're trying to definitively make a way that's clear and irrefutable makes sense to talk to this person this way based on how they filled out the app versus over here. This is also just a qualifying question."   [14:30]  📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ─────────────────────────────  TEXTO:  "Second Question - Financial Qualifier"   NÚMEROS:  Question #2 position, 3 answer options   VISUAL:  Estrutura de pergunta financeira mostrando três caminhos: 1) "Yes, I have the money" (verde), 2) "No, that's too expensive" (vermelho - broky bait), 3) "I can get approved for funding" (amarelo - rota para perguntas de crédito)   [14:35] "Now, second questions. So, second questions I like to have as the financial qualifier. I like to get right to the point with this one, but I just don't want it to be the first one."   [14:50] "The financial qualifier, the way I've always done it is, hey, this thing that we sell costs this on average. And then I don't just stop there. I usually extend it out a little bit and I say, 'Assuming we get onto the call and everything makes sense for you to buy, do you have this amount liquid or can you get approved for this amount via funding?'"   [15:15] "And then the answers are generally pretty simple. Yes, I have that amount. No, that is too expensive. Or I can get approved for funding."   [15:25]  📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ─────────────────────────────  TEXTO:  "Logic-Based Question Branching"   NÚMEROS:  3 answer paths with different routing   VISUAL:  Fluxograma de lógica condicional: "Yes" vai direto para próximas perguntas, "No, too expensive" vai para página de desqualificação (sem pixel), "Funding" vai para perguntas de crédito secundárias, setas coloridas mostrando cada caminho   [15:30] "If they press the I can get approved for funding option, we will have a logic-based question branch that occurs. So again, if they say I can get approved for funding, they'll get routed to a secondary question."   [15:45] "If they specifically say, well, no, I have the money, then great. We'll route that person past this question because they don't need to go to that question. And if they say no, that's too expensive. Okay, get fucked. We don't want to talk to you at all."   [16:05] "And again, this is so I can send the right people to the confirmation page where I am only sending back the qualified people to the pixel. That way, I know that my pixel is only going to go after more qualified people."   [16:25]  📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ─────────────────────────────  TEXTO:  "Credit Question Compliance Strategy"   NÚMEROS:  $5K/day ad spend threshold   VISUAL:  Comparação de abordagens de perguntas de crédito: lado esquerdo mostra pergunta direta (para baixo spend), lado direito mostra pergunta compliance-friendly (para alto spend), ícone de alerta de compliance   [16:30] "Now, let me just argue this from multiple perspectives real quick. If I say I can get approved for the funding, this follow-up question here is going to be specific to like almost like a credit application at that point."   [16:45] "Most of you have funding partners and you also know the criteria of what it takes to get approved or disapproved for those funding partners. Now, in some instances, depending on the amount you spend, you cannot ask credit-based questions."   [17:05] "Let me be very direct when I say this. If you spend less than 5K a day, you probably don't have to worry about this and you can ask a credit-based question. The credit based question is also buried in logic."   [17:25] "This question can be extremely direct. It's like our funding partners require you to have blank as your specific qualifying criteria there in order for you to get approved for funding. Will you get approved for funding based on having that criteria?"   [17:45]  📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ─────────────────────────────  TEXTO:  "Compliant Credit Question Example"   NÚMEROS:  N/A   VISUAL:  Box destacado com exemplo de pergunta compliance-friendly: "If you went to a car dealership and got approved for financing, would you get approved with favorable terms based on your current score?" - evita palavras "credit" e números específicos de score   [17:50] "So, if I can't literally ask, do you have this credit score or greater? Then what I can ask instead is if you went to a car dealership and got approved for financing, would you get approved with favorable terms based on your current score?"   [18:10] "I avoided using the word credit. I avoided using the words specifically a numbered score. That's a credit score to be clear. And I gave an example where anybody who's not stupid would say, 'Okay, I understand what you're asking right here.'"   [18:30]  📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ─────────────────────────────  TEXTO:  "Question 3+ Framework - Sales Ammo"   NÚMEROS:  Questions 3, 4, 5+   VISUAL:  Arsenal de munição de vendas mostrando perguntas adicionais como balas, cada uma conectada a um objetivo específico de vendas, ícone de alvo representando uso estratégico de dados   [18:35] "Now, from here, this is very important to understand. Sometimes these are literally the only two questions you need. The first question is something easy to answer, something that still gives you leverage in your sales process."   [18:55] "Second question is always the financial qualifier. And from here, I want to be very direct when I say this. You could directly take them over to the name, email, phone number section if you want."   [19:10] "In some instances, it makes sense to ask additional questions. Again, the additional questions that you choose to ask from here should all be something that gives your sales team ammunition."   [19:25] "So, the third questions onward. So any question from here should be sales ammo. Sales ammo is you talk to your sales team and you determine what specifically they need or they want to know that you can provide to them through application answers."   [19:45] "I want to be very direct when I say this, not trying to be disrespectful towards sales people, but it's just important to note. Some salespeople are fucking stupid with the questions they want answered."   [20:00] "Like some of them will say things like, 'Uh, yeah, I just want to know if they have the money and if they want to do it now.' In other instances they have really good questions where they actually reflect on their sales process."   [20:20]  📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ─────────────────────────────  TEXTO:  "Andrew's Offer - Residential Property Question Analysis"   NÚMEROS:  N/A   VISUAL:  Exemplo específico mostrando diferença entre tipos de propriedade: comercial vs residencial vs wholesale vs short-term/long-term rentals, ícone de casa destacado para residencial, checkmark verde indicando melhor fit para conversão   [20:25] "Andrew can notice when he talks to his salespeople that his salespeople are consistently closing people who not only have already done a few real estate transactions, but also have specifically done residential real estate transactions."   [20:45] "It's totally different if somebody's done commercial real estate or if somebody's done wholesaling versus if they've literally actively owned like short-term rentals or long-term rentals and already own a home they could potentially convert."   [21:05] "If somebody already owned a fleet of residential homes that they could just convert into sober living homes, that'd save the person a lot of money on the acquisition part of the sober living home, would it not?"   [21:25]  📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ─────────────────────────────  TEXTO:  "Application vs Sales Call Question Decision"   NÚMEROS:  Poll results showing 1s vs 2s   VISUAL:  Balança comparando prós e contras: lado esquerdo "Add to Application" (leverage em automação, dados antecipados para vendedores) vs lado direito "Ask on Sales Call" (menos fricção, custo por call mais baixo)   [21:30] "Does that question need to be added to the application or can that question just be asked on a sales call? What do you guys think? Put a one in the chat if you think it should be added to the application. Put a two in the chat if you think they should just ask that on the sales call."   [21:50] "We got a lot of ones in the chat. Couple twos. So, here's the upside to adding that to the question. Here's the downside of adding it to the question."   [22:05] "The upside of adding that to the question is we can leverage it in our marketing automation. We can leverage it in a contextual hammer them sequence to that person and our salespeople know ahead of time that they can immediately just start talking about it."   [22:25] "The downside is it adds friction to the application which could increase our cost per call artificially as a result. And it's a simple question."   [22:40]  📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ─────────────────────────────  TEXTO:  "Logic Branching Power"   NÚMEROS:  Yes/No follow-up question   VISUAL:  Fluxo de lógica condicional mostrando pergunta inicial "Do you own real estate?" seguida de pergunta contextual "Do you own residential real estate?" apenas para quem respondeu "Yes", minimizando fricção enquanto captura dados valiosos   [22:45] "When you use the right applications, you have logic that you can use. So, if somebody says, 'Yes, I currently own real estate.' You can literally just follow it up with a contextual question for that person and say, 'Do you own residential real estate that you're considering converting to sober living homes?'"   [23:05] "Really simple yes or no question. Does that really add a lot of friction? Does that really dramatically increase the probability his cost per call is going to be higher? No. It's a fucking yes or no multiple choice question."   [23:25]  📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ─────────────────────────────  TEXTO:  "Critical Question Framework"   NÚMEROS:  3 criteria for adding questions   VISUAL:  Checklist com três critérios obrigatórios: 1) Will be leveraged in sales process (ícone de telefone), 2) Will be leveraged in marketing automation (ícone de email), 3) Is a qualifier for pixel routing (ícone de pixel), X vermelho se nenhum critério for atendido   [23:30] "What we're talking about right now, I want to be very direct on. You're sitting here right now and you're learning things that you could do that could improve your application process. Are you actually going to do them?"   [23:50] "If you are not going to leverage the data in your sales process, then don't ask the question. If you are not going to leverage the data in your marketing automation, then don't ask the question."   [24:10] "If the question is not a qualifier question that's going to route the person to the confirmation page that gets pixeled versus the confirmation page that doesn't get pixeled, then don't ask the question. Otherwise, you're just artificially increasing your cost per call."   [24:30]  📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ─────────────────────────────  TEXTO:  "Secret Shopping Your Sales Team"   NÚMEROS:  12 burner phones mentioned   VISUAL:  Ícone de telefone com lupa representando secret shopping, checklist de verificação: pre-call texts, pre-call emails, selfie video mentions, actual call usage of application data   [24:35] "If you've convinced yourself that your salespeople are actually using those questions, secret shop your sales team and find out. I counted the other day I have 12 burner phones currently that I use to secret shop sales teams that we work with."   [24:55] "I would love if there was a company that existed that solely just secret shops internet businesses sales processes. I think it'd be a killer company. I think all of us would pay for it."   [25:15] "If I answered a bunch of the sales ammo questions and I get no pre-call texts, I get no pre-call emails, the salesperson in their selfie video doesn't say anything about my application data and I get onto a sales call and they don't leverage the application data. Remove those fucking questions."   [25:40]  📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ─────────────────────────────  TEXTO:  "Leo's Pre-Call Video Strategy"   NÚMEROS:  N/A   VISUAL:  Exemplo de fluxo de comunicação pré-call: selfie video personalizado → referência a dados da aplicação → envio de conteúdo relevante → confirmação de preferência de comunicação (email vs texto)   [25:45] "Let me give some perspective on what Leo and I do in our sales process. When Leo sends over a selfie video to somebody that comes through our agency application, he'll literally look at the application data."   [26:05] "He'll be like, 'Hey, what's going on? I saw that you had this type of offer and that you're currently struggling with this. I'm actually going to send you over a video that Jeremy just made that specifically talks on what we can do to help you with that specific problem.'"   [26:25] "Do me a favor, shoot me over some times tomorrow that work for you to get on a call and we look forward to chat with you. Just confirm with me, by the way, if this is the place that you want me to send that video. Do you want me to email it or do you prefer that I text it to you?"   [26:50]  📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ─────────────────────────────  TEXTO:  "Andrew's Offer - Personalized Video Example"   NÚMEROS:  2 residential properties in Phoenix   VISUAL:  Script de vídeo personalizado mostrando como usar dados específicos: número de propriedades, localização, tipo de propriedade, intenção de conversão, com setas conectando cada dado a ponto específico do script   [26:55] "For Andrew's offer, imagine you're going through Andrew's offer to get into sober living homes and you already have done real estate deals and you already own two different residential properties. How fucking nice would it be to get a video from one of Andrew's salespeople."   [27:20] "'Hey, what's going on, Jeremy? I saw you already do real estate. That's awesome, by the way. And I see that you have two homes and it looks like they're located in Phoenix that you might want to convert into sober living homes.'"   [27:40] "'I've got a video from Andrew that directly talks about the criteria of how and what you need to convert an existing home you own into a sober living home. Do you mind if I text that over to you so you can watch it before you get on our call?'"   [28:00]  📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ─────────────────────────────  TEXTO:  "Alex & Frankie - Tree Service Business Segmentation"   NÚMEROS:  2 distinct segments: solo operators vs companies with employees   VISUAL:  Comparação visual de dois avatares: operador solo (uma pessoa com ferramenta) vs empresa com equipe (múltiplas pessoas, estrutura organizacional), problemas diferentes listados para cada segmento   [28:05] "Or like Alex and Frankie, they own a fucking agency that helps tree service businesses. You say that there's some that have employees and there's some that don't have employees, right?"   [28:20] "So, you got the solo guys versus the guys that are running companies. The operators, they have completely different problems in comparison to the ones that have employees and staff and probably aren't in the field anymore."   [28:40] "Wouldn't it make a lot of fucking sense to have sales assets, pre-call communication, videos as an example, that actively communicate about how you can help each one of those different demographics?"   [28:55] "And how much better do you think your sales calls would go as a result of doing that kind of stuff pre-call?"   [29:05] "Much better."   [29:10]  📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ─────────────────────────────  TEXTO:  "Contextual Communication Impact"   NÚMEROS:  N/A   VISUAL:  Conceito de personalização mostrando lead genérico transformando em lead engajado através de comunicação contextual, ícones de trust building, seta de progressão indicando aumento de probabilidade de fechamento   [29:15] "And again that's great that there's commonalities. There's always commonalities, but it's those little things that you use that are contextual to that person's circumstances that really make the person feel like you're going the extra mile."   [29:35] "It builds more trust when we know those problems that the higher level companies have."   [29:45]  📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ─────────────────────────────  TEXTO:  "Personal Video Impact - Testimonials"   NÚMEROS:  Multiple Inner Circle members received videos   VISUAL:  Testemunhos mostrando impacto de vídeos personalizados: Mason "closed within 5 seconds", Andrew "pushed me to jump in", demonstração de como vídeo contextual acelera decisão de compra   [29:50] "Do me a favor real quick. Put a one in the chat if you got a video text from me when you were going through a sales process for one of my offers."   [30:10] "Mason, unmute yourself real quick. Did that video actually make a difference in the sales process to you?"   [30:18] "Yeah, I think it was Leo sent over like a case study, maybe a few case studies and I watched them and basically closed within five seconds on the call because that particular thing had some kind of contextual meaning to you."   [30:40] "Andrew, you unmute yourself. What did I say in the video to you?"   [30:48] "You had context from like what I needed because you knew I needed closers and you mentioned that. I think you shot it on your balcony, dude."   [31:00] "I was walking on the balcony. I was telling you, I was like, 'Dude, closers are the easy part, you know, like you getting that done, then you unlock all this other shit you can do right after, right?'"   [31:15] "And then you just pushed me at the end like just jumped in, just do it. And I did."   [31:25] "And would you have pulled the trigger without that video or would you have just dragged the sales process out and maybe not have even bought?"   [31:35] "I was on the fence, but like and I see this in my offer too if I personally reach out to people like, 'Oh my gosh, it's the guy.' Like, 'Okay, I'm jumping in.' Like, 'It's real. It's not a scam.'"   [31:55]  📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ─────────────────────────────  TEXTO:  "Data Leverage Success Formula"   NÚMEROS:  N/A   VISUAL:  Fórmula simples: Application Data → Personalized Video → Increased Trust → Faster Close, ícones representando cada etapa, seta de crescimento indicando resultado positivo   [32:00] "And again, all we did in Andrew's process there was just leverage data that he gave us in a very simple video. And we knew we could help him, but again, he gave us the ammo that we needed to get him closed."   [32:15] "And look at Andrew now making a fuckload more money. Imagine if he didn't buy, he wouldn't have gone to Disney this week and made 40 grand. He'd probably still be on sales calls."   [32:35]  📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ─────────────────────────────  TEXTO:  "Question Framework Summary"   NÚMEROS:  Question 1, Question 2, Questions 3+   VISUAL:  Pirâmide de estrutura de perguntas: base = Q1 (easy answer, multiple choice), meio = Q2 (financial qualifier com broky bait), topo = Q3+ (sales ammo se usar os dados), cada nível codificado por cor   [32:40] "So my point remains the same. If you're not going to use the data from question three onward, you should not have that question there. If you are going to use the data, then you should have the question there."   [32:55] "Question one, easy to answer, gets them into the application flow. Multiple choice can still be a qualifier, but it's a simple question that's easy to answer."   [33:10] "Question two should be the financial qualifier. Yes, I have the money. No, it's too expensive. Broky bait. You want to make sure you have multiple instances throughout your application to bait the brokies out of being pixelled."   [33:30]  📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ─────────────────────────────  TEXTO:  "TypeForm + Calendly Dominance"   NÚMEROS:  $125 cost per call (TypeForm/Calendly) vs $190 cost per call (GoHighLevel), 50% drop-off when separated   VISUAL:  Comparação lado a lado: TypeForm+Calendly (vencedor com checkmark verde) vs outras soluções (GoHighLevel, HubSpot com X vermelho), gráfico mostrando diferença dramática de custo por call   [33:35] "And again, when you get to the point where you have the scheduler, you guys always hear me talk about Type Form and Calendly. Every single time I've used Type Form and Calendly, it outcompetes whatever the fuck else is out there currently."   [34:00] "To be extremely clear, I'm not endorsing this. I have not used it. I don't know anybody who's used it. It looks like it's brand fucking new. We're essentially at this stage of learning about it."   [34:20] "I don't give a singular fuck about type form and Calendly as the combo that you use besides the fact that it's consistently driven lower call cost and allows me to functionally do what I'm trying to do."   [34:40] "I care about conditional routing. I care about the application and scheduler being combined into the same step. Because again, when it comes to best practices, what I do not want is an application that gets completed that then pushes to a separate page that has the scheduler on it."   [35:00]  📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ─────────────────────────────  TEXTO:  "50% Drop-Off Problem"   NÚMEROS:  50% average drop when application and scheduler are separated   VISUAL:  Funil dramático mostrando perda de 50% de leads qualificados quando há separação entre aplicação e agendamento, seta vermelha descendente, comparação com 0% drop quando combinados   [35:05] "The reason being we see a 50% drop on average when somebody's qualified in the application not booking when the scheduler is separated from the application. That is literally damn near the only reason that I give a fuck about type form and Calendly."   [35:25] "I want the application and the scheduler to be combined into the same step. Why I like type form and Calendly as a combo is it functionally looks great and performs better in split tests against the other combos I've tried."   [35:45]  📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ─────────────────────────────  TEXTO:  "HubSpot Criticism"   NÚMEROS:  N/A   VISUAL:  Comparação negativa do HubSpot: interface desajeitada vs TypeForm clean, custo alto vs valor limitado, necessidade de customização HTML vs pronto para usar, X vermelho sobre HubSpot   [35:50] "Sometimes people bring up HubSpot. I think HubSpot's shit. I think I genuinely think makes no fucking sense to ever use HubSpot. I think it's just a clunky fucking piece of shit CRM."   [36:10] "I'd rather have people pay for go high level than pay for HubSpot. That's because almost everybody I've ever seen use HubSpot does not actually functionally use HubSpot for all of the available function it has."   [36:30] "They claim that they can do something like what type form and Calendly does, but it looks like archaic dog shit. And I've heard people argue with me because I say this where they're like, 'Oh, dude, you can get it to look better.'"   [36:50] "It's like, type form already looks better. I don't want to waste a fucking time to try to tweak HubSpot's shitty looking form through HTML to look better. I just want the form to already be better."   [37:10]  📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ─────────────────────────────  TEXTO:  "GoHighLevel Test Results"   NÚMEROS:  $125 vs $190 cost per call difference   VISUAL:  Resultados de teste A/B mostrando TypeForm/Calendly significativamente melhor que GoHighLevel em custo por call, gráfico de barras com diferença marcante, nota sobre design visual impactando conversão   [37:15] "I have just most recently as well been testing go high level against the type form Calendly combo. It's like functionally the same as the type form Calendly combo but the look of it is different."   [37:35] "I just want to be clear when I say this type form and calendar still wins when it comes to cost per call and it's not by like a marginal amount. It's not even fucking close."   [37:50] "If I get, let's say, $125 cost per call through type form and Calendly, which was literally the recent test result, I was getting $190 cost per result through the go high level thing with everything being the exact same."   [38:15] "I'm not opposed like this new iClosed.io thing. I have no fucking clue if this actually works or not. I'm not actively endorsing it as a potentially better option but like functionally it looks like it's going to do what type form does."   [38:40]  📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ─────────────────────────────  TEXTO:  "Josh Troy / Wires From Strangers Challenge"   NÚMEROS:  2x cost per call with ScheduleOnce, 50% efficiency loss   VISUAL:  Caso especial mostrando preferência de sales agency por ScheduleOnce apesar de dobrar custo por call, balança mostrando trade-off entre preferências de sistema vs eficiência de custo   [38:45] "My favorite sales team ever, my buddy Josh Troy, he has that company, Wires from Strangers. Literally my only gripe about Josh is they fucking hate Calendly and they only want to use schedule once."   [39:10] "My cost per call on any deal I do with Josh right off the rip, without exaggeration, I know that it's going to be double the cost per call that it should be because they will force me to use schedule once instead of Calendly."   [39:30] "Every single deal I do with them, I have to tell the client we are artificially doubling our cost per call as a result of this setup of using schedule once. It sucks, but it's true and it has to be acknowledged."   [39:50] "I could literally get twice the efficiency of my cost per call with the exact same spend if I could just use something that combines my application and my scheduler."   [40:10]  📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ─────────────────────────────  TEXTO:  "Max's Question - Application Depth vs Intent"   NÚMEROS:  3-4% in-market buyers, 30% convincible segments   VISUAL:  Estádio do Ultimate Selling Machine de Chet Holmes mostrando segmentação de mercado: 3-4% in-market (centro), múltiplos anéis de 30% em diferentes níveis de convencimento, estratégia hammer them targeting outer rings   [40:15] "Max: One of the things that we've noticed is like the more in-depth they fill out the application because we have questions where they can like type in responses usually the better they are."   [40:35] "Yeah, fair. Generally, the more friction that you add, the higher quality the lead's going to be if they take the time to actually answer those questions. But here's what I also want to make very clear. What you're articulating is intent."   [41:00] "If you remember from the ultimate selling machine by Chet Holmes, there's this stadium and you're the little speaker up here talking. In market is like the 3 to 4% of people that are already identifying with your problem."   [41:20] "Most people will specifically try to sell to the 3 to 4%. And when you get a lot of application data to create friction, all you're really doing is separating the people who kind of give a shit from the people who really give a shit."   [41:45]  📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ─────────────────────────────  TEXTO:  "The 30% Opportunity"   NÚMEROS:  3-4% in-market vs 30% outer rings, significantly more money in outer segments   VISUAL:  Diagrama comparativo mostrando tamanho de mercado: pequeno círculo de 3-4% vs grande anel de 30%, cifrões indicando potencial de receita maior nos segmentos externos   [41:50] "When you lose this specific demographic here, I mean, dude, there's a lot of money in this niche, but most businesses, they aren't built to convert that demographic."   [42:10] "This is where strategies like hammer them really come in because this demographic doesn't necessarily need something like hammer them because they're already in market."   [42:30] "There's a lot more money because there's 30% of the entire market in that specific niche versus there's only 3 to 4% of a specific market that's considered in market at any given time."   [42:50] "So you can't confuse that somebody who comes through with a little less intent is not probable to buy. They just require you to have a more sophisticated conversion mechanism for them such as adding in the hammer them."   [43:15]  📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ─────────────────────────────  TEXTO:  "Max's Urgency Question Analysis"   NÚMEROS:  Within a week, within a month, within a year timeframes   VISUAL:  Timeline mostrando diferentes níveis de urgência, conceito de opportunity cost sendo usado para acelerar decisão, setas mostrando como criar urgência em leads "within a year"   [43:20] "Max: Our first question is how many properties they own now. Then I guess my question because the only two ammo things I think the sales team would want would be a partner spouse and then like an urgency based question."   [43:45] "I do love urgency questions, but I don't necessarily use them as qualifiers unless they're really far out. If I have somebody who's like, I want to do this within a week, within a month, or within a year."   [44:10] "A lot of the times businesses will want me to disqualify the people who say within a year. But I believe in my capacity as a salesperson to convert that demographic into having more urgency through what I can put in front of them."   [44:35]  📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ─────────────────────────────  TEXTO:  "Opportunity Cost Sales Script"   NÚMEROS:  Lottery odds comparison, saturation timing   VISUAL:  Script de vendas mostrando como converter falta de urgência em urgência: analogia de loteria → conceito de saturação → timing de oportunidade, pontos de conversação destacados   [44:40] "Let me just ask you have you ever heard of opportunity cost? Do you ever go buy a lottery ticket Max? What's your chance of winning the lottery? Pretty fucking low, huh?"   [45:05] "Would you rather wait until this gets to that point or would you rather take advantage of it now rather than later? There's a lot of people out there who don't feel safe doing things until other people have officially saturated the opportunity."   [45:30] "Do you want to be one of the people that waits until it's saturated to feel safe doing this or would you rather, I'd love to go through some data of why it makes sense to do now rather than later."   [45:50]  📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ─────────────────────────────  TEXTO:  "Qualifier vs Sales Ammo Distinction"   NÚMEROS:  N/A   VISUAL:  Diagrama Venn mostrando overlap entre perguntas de qualificação e munição de vendas, com urgency question posicionada primariamente como sales ammo, não qualifier   [45:55] "Is that a qualifier question or is that a sales ammo question? To me it's a sales ammo question because that just gives me the ammo I need for what I need to communicate to you about on the sales call to get you to convert."   [46:15] "I don't view it as a qualifier, but I do love it to indicate to me how I need to communicate to you."   [46:30]  📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ─────────────────────────────  TEXTO:  "The 30% Opportunity - Don't Leave Money on Table"   NÚMEROS:  30% of convincible market being left behind   VISUAL:  Pilhas de dinheiro sendo deixadas para trás por businesses que só focam em in-market buyers, seta apontando para oportunidade perdida, conceito de adaptation sendo a chave   [46:35] "A lot of businesses will disqualify only seeking in-market people. I mean, dude, you're just leaving a fuckload of money on the table because you're not adapting your sales process to it and you're not allowing them to come through the application at all."   [47:00]  📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ─────────────────────────────  TEXTO:  "Lower Ticket Drop-Sell Strategy"   NÚMEROS:  N/A   VISUAL:  Fluxo de roteamento mostrando leads "too expensive" sendo direcionados para produto lower-ticket ou sales page direta, conceito de capturar receita de leads desqualificados sem pixelar   [47:05] "Question: So, I said on question two, if someone clicks no, it's too expensive, I've got this kind of lower ticket product that's essentially just like the modules and like no coaching. Would you send them straight from the application to like a sales page?"   [47:30] "Yeah, you absolutely could. If you want to get them on the phone still and drop sell them on something else, you could do that. If you prefer to send them straight to a sales page, you could do that."   [47:50]  📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ─────────────────────────────  TEXTO:  "Contact Information Timing & Partial Submit"   NÚMEROS:  N/A   VISUAL:  Fluxo de aplicação mostrando ordem correta: Q1 (easy) → Q2 (financial) → Q3+ (sales ammo) → Contact Info → Partial Submit → Calendly com campos pré-populados   [47:55] "Question: When do you ask for their contact information? Because right now my type form, I have it actually as the first question, which I know you said to not do."   [48:15] "You can have a it's called a partial submit. The partial submit will just capture their data before they continue from that point onward. After I ask my sales ammo questions, that's where I'm asking the personal information."   [48:40] "After that, that's when I'm letting them book a call. But I'll do a partial submit right before I allow them to book the call. That way, we get their information regardless in the instance that they do drop."   [49:00]  📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ─────────────────────────────  TEXTO:  "Field Pre-Population in Calendly"   NÚMEROS:  N/A   VISUAL:  Demonstração de merge fields: dados da TypeForm (nome, email, telefone) automaticamente populando campos do Calendly, ícone de "+" para adicionar merge fields, eliminação de re-digitação   [49:05] "I can prepopulate with the fields that they've provided me their information into that Calendly form. So they don't have to type anything. Literally, all they have to do is just select the call time and press confirm."   [49:25] "When you're in the type form itself and you are at the step where you're configuring Calendly, in the box where it says first name, last name, email, phone, there's a little plus icon on the right hand side of those boxes."   [49:45] "That's where it pulls up the merger fields from all the previous questions that they've answered that you can choose to input right in there automatically."   [50:05]  📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ─────────────────────────────  TEXTO:  "Multi-Rep Time Zone Management"   NÚMEROS:  6 different time zones mentioned   VISUAL:  Mapa mostrando múltiplos fusos horários, configuração de Calendly com múltiplos usuários, settings de work hours vs calendar availability, toggle de time zone adjustment   [50:10] "Question: With Calendly, can you have like multiple reps in different time zones on the same calendar?"   [50:20] "Yeah, I'm under the impression that you can. I've never had an issue with that. Each user that you invite in has the ability to select what times they're available like their blocked windows and their availability windows."   [50:45] "Each individual user that's invited to the account has whatever availability is defaulted into their calendar which is also defaulted to their individual time zones. I've had closers across I think six different time zones at the same time with no issues."   [51:15] "There's also setting when you're setting up the Calendly where it allows you to flick a toggle to adjust the time zone to that lead who's booking. So if I'm Eastern and I'm booking a call with you, it'll show me all the availability based on Eastern time zone."   [51:40]  📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ─────────────────────────────  TEXTO:  "Work Hours Override Issue"   NÚMEROS:  N/A   VISUAL:  Screenshot conceitual de settings do Calendly mostrando toggle entre "Use Google Calendar availability" vs "Use these work hours", destacando que work hours override calendar availability   [51:45] "Go into the user settings. There's a setting in Calendly that can override the Google calendar availability. It's called work hours and the work hours will override the Google calendar availability."   [52:10] "You likely have it set to use the work hours which is also what I'm pretty sure it defaults to by the way. So probably that's the error."   [52:30]  📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ─────────────────────────────  TEXTO:  "GoHighLevel Timeline vs Tags"   NÚMEROS:  Thousands of leads mentioned   VISUAL:  Comparação de tracking: GoHighLevel timeline (detalhado, mostra opens sem ação) vs Tag-based system (apenas ações completadas), conceito de segmentação via tags para comunicação contextual   [52:35] "Question: The other issue which made me go back to go high level was go high level provides all the user data like they viewed this landing page on this time with from this campaign from this ad."   [53:00] "Long story short you would still have visibility into that timeline, but you would have to adjust it to reflect with tags as an example. If they took the action, it would add to that timeline because that would reflect with a tag that was added."   [53:25] "It would not have visibility into like when they clicked and did not do something. A user should still be tagged for segment for best practices in CRM you know segmented so you can contextually communicate as a result of what they did or did not do."   [53:55]  📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ─────────────────────────────  TEXTO:  "The Useless Question Example"   NÚMEROS:  N/A   VISUAL:  Exemplo de pergunta ruim destacada com X vermelho: "Do you agree to take the call in a space with undivided attention?" - pode ser comunicado via automação ao invés de pergunta na aplicação   [54:00] "Question: Most applications I've seen is just a like, hey, do you agree to take the call in a space where with undivided attention? This is a Zoom call. Calls will be cancelled if you don't show up."   [54:20] "I think that's shit. And here's why it's like that's usually indicative that that business's salespeople suck ass. Like it's indicative that their marketing sucks. It's indicative their marketing automation sucks."   [54:45] "Can that just be something that's sent out via marketing automation? Can that be something that your salespeople use pre-call? Why does that have to be a question? It just artificially drives up your cost per call."   [55:10]  📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ─────────────────────────────  TEXTO:  "iClosed.io Fatal Flaw"   NÚMEROS:  N/A   VISUAL:  Screenshot conceitual mostrando problema do iClosed.io: obriga captura de phone/email ANTES de qualificar/desqualificar, X vermelho gigante, "TypeForm/Calendly still undefeated"   [55:15] "Alexander puts in the chat with this iClosed.io shit: You have to ask for phone and email before you qualified as disqualified. Yeah, super whack. Fuck that. I think that gives us the answer we need on that software."   [55:35] "Type form Calendly for the dub. It's just fucking undefeated. I have no idea why. I just don't get how fucking companies can't compete with this shit. Makes honestly makes no sense to me."   [55:55]  📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ─────────────────────────────  TEXTO:  "Christian's GoHighLevel Demo - Separated Pages Issue"   NÚMEROS:  50% drop-off reminder   VISUAL:  Fluxo de Christian mostrando landing page → survey (todas perguntas visíveis de uma vez) → página separada de booking, highlighting do problema de separação e todas perguntas reveladas   [56:00] "Christian: I can share it with you on how it looks for GoHighLevel. So, the first page here, just the landing page, you hit the schedule to the demo. It comes to the application."   [56:25] "You'll for sure experience the drop off because they're separate. The other reason, and this is why I fucking hate HubSpot, you see how all your questions here are revealed already. That's what drops off a lot of users."   [56:50] "Type form and to be fair, Jotform as well does this. Where it shows like a singular question at a time that tremendously increases application retention where you only show like a singular question at a time rather than all of them at once."   [57:15] "Christian with that process, I'm not exaggerating, dude. Every single time at scale that we separate the application from the scheduler, sometimes it's worse than a 50% drop. Sometimes it's marginally better, but it always averages to 50%."   [57:40]  📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ─────────────────────────────  TEXTO:  "Setter Team Exception"   NÚMEROS:  2x artificial cost per call increase   VISUAL:  Cenário especial mostrando como setter team forte pode mitigar problema de separação, capturando drops qualificados, mas ainda resultando em dobro do custo por call e perda de pixel conditioning   [57:45] "If you have a really good setter team that can capture the people who drop off that were qualified that did not schedule, you can still benefit from doing the separated preferential software of your choice and just endure the drop off."   [58:10] "Like with Josh's team with the wires from strangers examples, they have a really good setter team and they still capture those people. So it doesn't piss me off as much."   [58:30] "It just drives up my cost per call artificially and it doesn't allow my qualified people to hit my pixel which pisses me off more than anything else."   [58:50]  📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ─────────────────────────────  TEXTO:  "Best Practices Summary - Non-Negotiables"   NÚMEROS:  2 critical requirements   VISUAL:  Checklist final com dois requisitos absolutos: 1) Application + Scheduler COMBINED (ícone de integração), 2) One question at a time revealed (ícone de questão única), software agnóstico desde que atenda esses critérios   [58:55] "All I care about I don't care what software. I just want the two to be combined and ideally I want it to be like one question at a time for what's revealed. That's the best practices I'm trying to articulate."   [59:15] "Whatever software you choose, just make sure that it does those things and you'll have a very high probability to replicate the success of the type form Calendly combo."   [59:35] "That's what makes it good. One question at a time that they see and the application and calendar are integrated together. That's it."   [59:55]  📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ─────────────────────────────  TEXTO:  "GoHighLevel Survey + HTML Embed Workaround"   NÚMEROS:  N/A   VISUAL:  Tutorial conceitual: setup de GoHighLevel survey → última pergunta = HTML embed do calendar → não configurar redirect no calendar (senão não submete application), workaround para combinar survey + scheduler   [01:00:00] "Nick in chat: With go high level, you set up as a survey. Then in the survey, the last question you'll just embed an HTML, which is the calendar. So just one survey question at a time."   [01:00:20] "On the last survey question, you'll embed the HTML code from go high level. You just want to make sure that the calendar is not set to a redirect page because they're not going to submit the application if that happens."   [01:00:40] "Can you make a loom on that and put it in the group chat? Yeah, I'll film that."   [01:01:00] "All right. Cool. All right, everybody. Good call today. Hope it helped you. Let's go. Let's go get rich here. All right. Talk soon."     📊 RESUMO EXECUTIVO   NÚMEROS PRINCIPAIS: 50% drop-off médio quando application e scheduler são separados 3.3x maior taxa de conversão para leads com propriedades vs sem propriedades (caso Andrew) $125 custo por call (TypeForm/Calendly) vs $190 (GoHighLevel) 2x aumento artificial no custo por call quando usando ScheduleOnce 40% dos leads de Andrew têm zero propriedades 46% dos leads da Evoke Advisors têm $5M+ (de $23B AUM total) 12 telefones burner usados para secret shopping 6 fusos horários diferentes gerenciados simultaneamente 3-4% do mercado está "in-market" vs 30% em segmentos convencíveis Menos de $5K/dia spend = pode fazer perguntas diretas de crédito   ESTRATÉGIAS E SISTEMAS: Application Question Framework:  Q1 (easy answer, multiple choice, sales leverage), Q2 (financial qualifier com "broky bait"), Q3+ (sales ammo apenas se usar dados) Logic-Based Branching:  Rotear leads qualificados vs desqualificados para diferentes confirmation pages (pixeled vs non-pixeled) Pixel Conditioning Strategy:  Apenas enviar leads qualificados de volta ao pixel para treinar algoritmo Partial Submit:  Capturar dados antes do agendamento para minimizar perda Pre-Populated Fields:  Merge fields do TypeForm auto-preenchem Calendly Drop-Sell Strategy:  Leads "too expensive" vão para lower-ticket offers ou sales pages diretas Secret Shopping Protocol:  Testar se sales team realmente usa application data Contextual Communication:  Email sequences e hammer them content baseados em respostas específicas Pre-Call Video Strategy:  Selfie videos personalizados referenciando application data específica Multi-Tier Segmentation:  Diferentes sales processes para diferentes níveis de qualificação   ESTRUTURA: Evoke Advisors Tiers:  $1M, $5M, $10M, $25M, $50M, $100M+ (diferentes comunicações para cada) Andrew's Property Segmentation:  0, 1-5, 6+ properties (deveria ser 0, 1-2, 3-5 para melhor targeting) Alex & Frankie Tree Service:  Solo operators vs companies com employees James' Alcohol-Free Tiers:  $25K, $15K, $5K offers (captura $30K/semana de "unqualified") Chet Holmes Stadium Model:  3-4% in-market center, múltiplos 30% rings de convincible prospects TypeForm + Calendly Integration:  Singular question reveal, combined app+scheduler, conditional routing GoHighLevel Workaround:  Survey format → HTML embed calendar na última pergunta → no redirect setting Credit Question Compliance:  Direct (sub-$5K/day) vs Indirect car dealership analogy (over $5K/day)   DADOS FINANCEIROS: $125 vs $190 cost per call (TypeForm vs GoHighLevel) = 52% mais eficiente 50% dos leads qualificados perdidos = dobro do custo efetivo quando separado Andrew fez $40K durante Disney trip (resultado de comprar Inner Circle) James' team fez $30K+ em uma semana apenas de $5K "unqualified" sales $23 billion AUM gerenciado pela Evoke Advisors $1M+ minimum para Evoke Advisors 2x cost per call artificial com ScheduleOnce vs Calendly Spending $15-20K/day (James' team low end) ROI de liquidação através de lower-ticket offers para desqualificados   DURAÇÃO:  61 minutos  FORMATO:  Weekly Inner Circle training call sobre Application Funnel Best Practices
